data.title: Is It Time To Reconsider Dutch Bros (BROS) After Recent Share Price Pullback
data.description:
• Wondering if Dutch Bros at around US$48.17 is a fair price or an opportunity? This article walks through what the numbers actually say about its value.
  • The share price has seen mixed results, with a 3 year return of 59.7% alongside declines of 11.6% over 7 days, 12.5% over 30 days, 22.5% year to date, and 19.2% over 1 year.
  • Recent headlines have focused on Dutch Bros as a growing coffee chain listed on the NYSE under the ticker BROS and on the market's changing expectations around its growth plans and store expansion pace. These stories help explain why sentiment has swung over shorter periods, even while the longer term return picture looks different.
  • On our framework, Dutch Bros has a valuation score of 1 out of 6. Next we will walk through what that means across different valuation methods and why there might be an even more useful way to think about value by the end of this article.

Dutch Bros scores just 1/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Dutch Bros Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today using a required rate of return, to arrive at an estimate of what the business may be worth per share.

For Dutch Bros, the model used here is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The company’s last twelve months Free Cash Flow is reported at about $4.0 million. Analyst and extrapolated projections, provided in the model, increase this over time, with Free Cash Flow for 2029 projected at $194.9 million and further values estimated out to 2035.

When all these projected cash flows are discounted back to today in US$, the DCF model produces an estimated intrinsic value of about $31.21 per share. Compared with the recent share price of around $48.17, this implies the stock is about 54.3% overvalued based on this cash flow outlook.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Dutch Bros may be overvalued by 54.3%. Discover 47 high quality undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Dutch Bros.

Approach 2: Dutch Bros Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies like Dutch Bros, the P/E ratio is a useful shortcut because it links what you pay today to the earnings the business is already generating. The “right” P/E is not a fixed number; it usually reflects what the market thinks about a company’s growth prospects and risk, with higher growth or lower perceived risk often justifying a higher P/E.

Dutch Bros is currently trading on a P/E of 76.7x. That sits above the Hospitality industry average of 21.3x and also above the peer group average of 56.4x, so on simple comparisons the shares are priced more expensively than many similar companies.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio framework goes a step further. It estimates what a reasonable P/E might be for Dutch Bros, given factors such as its earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and company specific risks. For Dutch Bros, this Fair Ratio is 32.9x, which is well below the current P/E of 76.7x. Compared with a tailored Fair Ratio, the stock screens as expensive on this metric.

Result: OVERVALUED

data.title: Natera Launches Zenith Genomics To Expand Rare Disease Testing Story
data.description:
• Natera (NasdaqGS:NTRA) has launched Zenith Genomics, a next generation whole genome sequencing assay for rare disease detection.
  • The assay is being introduced in partnership with MyOme, with exclusive collaboration around genome wide rare disease diagnostics.
  • Zenith Genomics integrates advanced methylation analysis with DNA sequencing to address difficult diagnostic questions in rare conditions.
  • The launch and supporting data are being presented at a leading medical genetics conference, targeting clinicians focused on complex hereditary disease.

Natera is already known for its genetic testing portfolio across oncology, women’s health and organ health. Zenith Genomics adds a whole genome rare disease offering to that mix. The assay uses both DNA and epigenetic information, which is designed to help clinicians address cases where standard sequencing has not provided clear answers. For investors, it represents another technology pillar tied to specialist clinical use.

The MyOme partnership and planned electronic medical record integration point to a model that aims to fit into everyday clinical workflows rather than remaining confined to research settings. As Natera references rapid expansion of actionable disease targets, you may want to watch how quickly the test menu develops and how adoption progresses among geneticists and rare disease centers.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Natera by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Natera.

3 things going right for Natera that this headline doesn't cover.

This launch is really about Natera trying to broaden its role in high complexity genomics by pairing MyOme’s multi omic platform with its own commercial reach. Zenith genomics sits alongside Signatera and the women’s and organ health tests, giving the company another use case where deep sequencing and interpretation matter. The focus on long read sequencing, methylation signatures and whole genome coverage speaks directly to rare disease cases where standard panels have not delivered clear diagnoses, which is a pain point for clinicians and health systems facing high rare disease costs.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Natera Narrative

  • Zenith supports the existing narrative that new product launches and AI oriented workflows can expand Natera’s footprint in personalized medicine and help justify its investment in genomics infrastructure.
  • The collaboration with MyOme could also stretch resources, as Natera is already funding trials and commercialization in oncology and organ health, so investors may want to watch how operating spend tracks against new revenue sources.
  • The rare disease whole genome focus and integrated methylation readout are not front and center in many oncology heavy narratives, so the potential role of this business line may be underappreciated in some storylines.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Natera to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Integrating a premium genome test into routine care depends on payer policies for comprehensive sequencing, so any pushback on reimbursement could limit volume despite clinical interest.
  • ⚠️ Competition from other genomics players such as Invitae and GeneDx, and from broader whole genome platforms at companies like Illumina’s partners, may keep pricing and margins under pressure in rare disease testing.
  • 🎁 If Zenith shortens diagnostic timelines for complex conditions, it could strengthen Natera’s relationships with geneticists and tertiary centers, supporting cross selling of its broader test menu.
  • 🎁 Automatic methylation analysis on eligible exome and genome orders from early Q2 2026 may increase test utility for clinicians without adding workflow friction, which can be a practical advantage when health systems standardize vendors.

What To Watch Going Forward

You may want to track a few things from here. First, how quickly Zenith is adopted across Natera’s existing electronic medical record integrations and whether ordering becomes routine at major rare disease centers. Second, watch for any updates on payer coverage criteria for whole genome and methylation supported testing, since those policies will influence test economics. Finally, compare Natera’s rare disease progress with updates from peers like Invitae and GeneDx to gauge how competitive its offering appears in terms of clinical utility and workflow.

Approach 1: eBay Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today, so you can compare that value to the current share price.

For eBay, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach that works off the company’s latest twelve month free cash flow of about $1.45b. Analysts provide explicit free cash flow estimates for several years, and Simply Wall St then extends those out to 10 years using its own assumptions, with projected free cash flow reaching about $3.43b in 2030. All of these future cash flows, shown in the ten year projections, are discounted back into today’s dollars in the model.

On this basis, the DCF model suggests an intrinsic value of about $118.86 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $90.00, this implies eBay trades at around a 24.3% discount to that estimate, which points to the shares looking undervalued on this particular cash flow view.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests eBay is undervalued by 24.3%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 47 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for eBay.

Approach 2: eBay Price vs Earnings

For a profitable business like eBay, the P/E ratio is a useful way to see what you are paying for each dollar of current earnings. It also loosely reflects what the market expects in terms of future growth and how much risk investors see in those earnings.

Higher growth and lower perceived risk usually support a higher, or more premium, P/E, while lower growth or higher risk point to a lower, more conservative, multiple. eBay currently trades on a P/E of 20.20x. That sits slightly above the Multiline Retail industry average of 19.16x, and well below the peer group average of 62.74x, which can sometimes be skewed by a few very highly valued companies.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for eBay is 19.99x. This is its proprietary view of what a reasonable P/E could be, based on factors like earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and specific risk profile. Because it is tailored to the company, it can be more informative than a simple comparison with peers or the broad industry.

With eBay’s actual P/E at 20.20x versus a Fair Ratio of 19.99x, the stock looks about in line with this earnings based yardstick.

Approach 2: Zalando Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Zalando, the P/E ratio is a handy shorthand for how much investors are paying for each euro of earnings. It ties the share price directly to the bottom line, which is usually what ultimately matters to shareholders.

What counts as a "normal" P/E depends a lot on what investors expect for future growth and how much risk they see in the business. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk often points to a lower one.

Zalando currently trades on a P/E of 23.27x. That sits close to both its peer average of 23.05x and above the Specialty Retail industry average of 15.19x. Simply Wall St also calculates a Fair Ratio of 17.28x, which is the P/E it would expect for Zalando after factoring in elements like earnings growth, industry, profit margin, market cap and company specific risks.

This Fair Ratio can be more informative than a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for those business specific factors rather than assuming all retailers deserve similar multiples. When set against the current 23.27x P/E, the 17.28x Fair Ratio indicates that Zalando is trading at a richer level than this framework would suggest.
